content.method: Instrumental-variable or regression-discontinuity design exploiting geographic variation in track availability and admission cutoffs, combined with decomposition analysis (e.g., variance decomposition separating within-settlement school-choice effects from track-structure effects) using existing PISA/national assessment microdata merged with settlement-level demographic data.
content.question: What is the causal contribution of the 6/8-year track structure itself, as distinct from free school choice, residential/ethnic segregation, and church/private admissions exemptions, to Hungary's exceptionally high between-school variance and segregation levels?
content.why_it_matters: Nearly every reform proposal (delaying selection, admissions reform, quality investment, partial phase-down, fiscal conditionality) implicitly assumes the track structure is a primary driver of segregation. If most segregation stems from residential sorting or unconstrained choice rather than tracking per se, structural reforms will underperform expectations and effort should redirect toward choice/catchment and residential levers.
